questions.question: Why does Temporal recommend making Activities idempotent?

choices.text: So that Temporal can compress the Event History and reduce storage costs over time
Because Activities may be retried automatically, and running them more than once should not cause unintended side effects
Because Workflows cannot track the results of Activities that run more than a single time
So that multiple Workers can execute the same Activity simultaneously without coordinating with each other

questions.explanation: Temporal automatically retries failed Activities, which means the same Activity function may run more than once. If an Activity is not idempotent — meaning running it multiple times produces different results — you can end up with problems like duplicate charges or extra records created. Idempotency ensures that no matter how many times the Activity runs, the end result is the same as if it had run just once. This is one of the key design principles that makes Temporal safe to use for real-world business logic.

questions.question: What is the difference between an Activity Definition and an Activity Execution in Temporal?

choices.text: An Activity Definition is the reusable code that describes what an Activity does, while an Activity Execution is a specific instance of that code actually running
An Activity Definition runs inside a Worker, while an Activity Execution runs directly inside the Temporal Service without a Worker
An Activity Definition is only used during local development, while an Activity Execution is the production-ready version of the same code
An Activity Definition stores the result of a completed task, while an Activity Execution holds the configuration settings for retries and timeouts

questions.explanation: Think of an Activity Definition like a recipe — it describes the steps to follow. An Activity Execution is what happens when you actually cook the meal. In Temporal, the Definition is the code you write (the function or method), and the Execution is a live instance of that function being run by a Worker. One Definition can be the basis for many Executions over the lifetime of your application.

questions.question: Why should large pieces of work in Temporal be broken into multiple smaller Activities rather than one big Activity?

choices.text: Temporal's Task Queue system can only handle messages below a fixed size limit, so large Activities are rejected at the network level
Using more Activities reduces the total number of events written to the Workflow's Event History, which lowers Temporal Service overhead
Smaller Activities make failure recovery easier because only the failed step needs to retry, each step can have a short timeout, and each step can be made idempotent on its own
Temporal Workers can only execute one Activity at a time, so smaller Activities allow other Workers to pick up remaining work in parallel

questions.explanation: When you put many steps into a single Activity and that Activity fails partway through, the entire Activity — including already-completed steps — must retry from the beginning. By splitting work into smaller Activities, only the step that actually failed needs to be retried. Smaller Activities also make it easier to set tight timeouts and to design each step to be idempotent. This granularity is a core pattern for building reliable, recoverable workflows in Temporal.

questions.question: A payment processing Activity in your Temporal workflow occasionally fails due to network timeouts and gets retried automatically. After reviewing your logs, you notice some customers are being charged twice. What is the most appropriate way to prevent this while keeping Temporal's retry behavior?

choices.text: Set the Activity's retry policy to max_attempts=1 so that the payment Activity never retries, relying on the Workflow to handle failures manually with compensating logic.
Catch all exceptions inside the Activity and return a success result anyway, preventing Temporal from ever seeing a failure and triggering a retry.
Pass an idempotency key (such as a combination of the Workflow Run ID and Activity ID) to the payment provider API, so that the provider ignores duplicate requests for the same transaction.
Split the Activity into smaller Activities so each step is shorter, ensuring that each individual step is unlikely to fail and therefore unlikely to be retried.

questions.explanation: The correct approach is to use an idempotency key derived from the Workflow Run ID and Activity ID when calling the payment provider. Since these values are stable across retry attempts but unique across Workflow Executions, the payment provider can detect and ignore duplicate requests. This is the pattern Temporal recommends: idempotency is enforced by the external service you're calling, not by Temporal itself. Option A prevents retries entirely, sacrificing Temporal's durability guarantees. Option B hides real failures from Temporal and could cause your Workflow to incorrectly proceed as though the payment succeeded when it didn't. Option D reduces retry frequency but doesn't prevent duplicate charges if a retry does occur — splitting Activities is good for granularity but doesn't solve the idempotency problem on its own.

questions.question: Which statement accurately describes the relationship between an Activity Execution and Activity Task Executions in Temporal?

choices.text: An Activity Execution and an Activity Task Execution are interchangeable terms; each invocation of the Activity function creates exactly one of each, with a one-to-one mapping between them.
An Activity Execution is the full chain of Activity Task Executions, meaning a single Activity Execution may involve multiple Task Executions if retries occur.
An Activity Task Execution encompasses multiple Activity Executions, so one Task Execution can spawn several independent Activity Executions across different Workers.
An Activity Execution refers only to the initial attempt of an Activity, while Activity Task Executions track all subsequent retry attempts separately in the Event History.

questions.explanation: An Activity Execution is the full chain of Activity Task Executions. This means that when an Activity is retried due to a failure or timeout, each retry is a new Activity Task Execution, but they all belong to the same Activity Execution. This distinction matters when reasoning about timeouts: for example, a ScheduleToClose timeout bounds the entire Activity Execution (all attempts), while a StartToClose timeout bounds each individual Task Execution. Options A and D incorrectly treat the terms as either equivalent or inverted. Option C has the hierarchy backwards — Task Executions are the atomic units within a single Activity Execution, not containers for multiple independent executions.

questions.question: Your long-running Activity needs to support cancellation when a user requests it from the Workflow. You've implemented cancellation handling in the Activity code, but cancellation requests are never being received. What is the most likely missing piece?

choices.text: The Activity must be registered on a separate Task Queue from the Workflow, because cancellation signals are only routed to Activities on dedicated Task Queues.
The Activity's retry policy must have a MaximumAttempts greater than 1, since cancellation can only be delivered to Activities that are eligible for retry.
The Workflow must call a special CancelWorkflow API rather than canceling the individual Activity, because per-Activity cancellation is not supported in Temporal.
The Activity must be sending regular Heartbeats to the Temporal Service, because Activities only receive cancellation requests when they heartbeat.

questions.explanation: Activities must heartbeat to receive cancellation requests from the Temporal Service. Without periodic heartbeats, the server has no mechanism to deliver a cancellation to a running Activity. Heartbeating is also what allows the server to detect that an Activity is still making progress. When the Workflow (or the server due to a timeout) requests cancellation, the Activity learns about it on its next heartbeat call. Option A is incorrect — Task Queue assignment has no bearing on cancellation delivery. Option B is wrong — retry eligibility is unrelated to cancellation. Option C is false — Temporal fully supports per-Activity cancellation, and cancelling a Workflow may cascade cancellation to its Activities, but you can also cancel individual Activities without cancelling the whole Workflow.

questions.question: When designing a Temporal Activity that performs three sequential steps — a database lookup, a microservice call, and writing results to a filesystem — what approach best balances idempotency and observability?

choices.text: Implement all three steps in a single Activity but use try/catch blocks around each step to skip already-completed steps on retry, keeping the Event History compact.
Place all three steps inside the Workflow function directly, since Workflow code is replayed deterministically and each step will only execute once regardless of failures.
Split the logic into three separate Activities, one per step, so that only the failing step is retried and each step's result is individually recorded in the Event History.
Combine all three steps into one Activity and set a very long StartToClose timeout to ensure the entire sequence has time to complete before Temporal considers it failed.

questions.explanation: Splitting the logic into three separate Activities is the recommended approach. If the third step (filesystem write) fails, only that Activity is retried — not the database lookup or microservice call. This makes each step individually idempotent and easier to debug, and gives you clear visibility in the Event History for each step's outcome. Option A puts retry-skip logic inside the Activity itself, which is fragile, hides failures from Temporal's metrics, and makes debugging harder in the Temporal UI — the documentation explicitly cautions against internal retries within Activities. Option B is wrong because Activities should handle side effects; placing I/O operations directly in Workflow code violates determinism requirements and won't replay safely. Option D keeps all steps in one Activity and relies on a long timeout, which means any single step failure retries all three steps, risking duplicate microservice calls and making it harder to identify which step actually failed.

questions.question: A payment processing workflow runs an Activity that (1) looks up a customer record, (2) calls a payment processor API, and (3) writes a receipt to a database. The payment processor API accepts an idempotency key. In production you observe occasional duplicate charges after Activity retries. Which approach best fixes the duplicate-charge problem while keeping the Activity atomic from Temporal's perspective?

choices.text: Split the single Activity into three separate Activities — one per step — so that only the failed step reruns on retry, eliminating the chance of a duplicate API call reaching the payment processor.
Set the Activity Retry Policy max_attempts to 1 so that the payment Activity never retries, preventing any duplicate charge; handle payment failures at the Workflow level by re-scheduling manually.

Pass a composite idempotency key (Workflow Run ID + Activity ID) to the payment processor API call inside the Activity, so the payment processor deduplicates repeat requests even when the entire Activity reruns from scratch on retry.
Wrap the three steps in a database transaction inside the Activity so that partial failures roll back atomically, ensuring the payment API is only called once per successful transaction commit.

questions.explanation: When an Activity Worker crashes after the payment API call succeeds but before the receipt is written, Temporal has no record of completion and will retry the entire Activity from step 1. The payment processor API will be called again with the same parameters. The correct fix is to pass an idempotency key — specifically the Workflow Run ID combined with the Activity ID, which Temporal guarantees to be stable across retries but unique across Workflow Executions — to the payment processor. The processor then deduplicates the second call server-side. Splitting into three Activities (A) reduces retry scope but doesn't prevent the payment API from being called again if only the third step fails; you'd still need an idempotency key for the API call. Disabling retries (B) sacrifices Temporal's durable execution guarantee entirely. A database transaction (D) can't make an external HTTP call idempotent — if the payment API call succeeds and then the transaction rolls back, you've charged the customer with no local record.

questions.question: A long-running Activity is processing a large media transcoding job. The Worker hosting it is gracefully shut down mid-execution. You check the Temporal Web UI and see the Activity is still in a 'Running' state even though no Worker is actively executing it. How does Temporal eventually detect that this Activity Task Execution is lost and trigger a retry?

choices.text: Temporal detects the Worker TCP connection drop immediately via its gRPC health-check stream and marks the Activity Task as failed, scheduling a retry within seconds of the disconnection.
The Temporal Service polls all registered Workers on a fixed 30-second interval; when the Worker is absent from two consecutive polls, the Activity Task is considered lost and rescheduled.
The Workflow's Schedule-To-Close timeout fires first, failing the entire Activity Execution rather than retrying it, because Temporal has no mechanism to detect individual Task loss mid-execution.

Temporal relies on the Start-To-Close timeout: once that interval elapses with no ActivityTaskCompleted or heartbeat from the Worker, the Activity Task is considered timed out and the Activity Execution is retried per its Retry Policy.

questions.explanation: Temporal does not detect Worker crashes directly. It has no polling mechanism to probe Workers, and a dropped connection does not instantly surface as a task failure at the server. Instead, Temporal uses the Start-To-Close timeout as its dead-man's switch: if the server does not receive an ActivityTaskCompleted event (or a heartbeat, if configured) within that window, it assumes the task is lost and retries according to the Activity Execution's Retry Policy. This is why setting an appropriate Start-To-Close timeout is listed as a required best practice — too long a value means you'll wait that entire duration before a retry is scheduled. Option A is incorrect because gRPC disconnection is not wired to immediate task failure. Option B describes a mechanism Temporal does not implement. Option C confuses Schedule-To-Close (the outer envelope timeout for the whole execution chain) with the per-task detection mechanism.

questions.question: Your team ships a new Worker binary that changes the behavior of an Activity but not its Activity Type name. Shortly after deploy, you notice that some in-flight Workflow Executions are getting ActivityTaskFailed events while others complete successfully. Heartbeat details from previous attempts are present in the Event History. Which statement best explains why heartbeat details matter here, and what guarantee they provide across retries?

choices.text: Heartbeat details are stored only in Worker memory; after the Worker restart they are lost, so any retry starts from the Activity's initial state regardless of the previous attempt's progress.

Heartbeat details are persisted on the Temporal Service and can be retrieved at the start of a new Activity Task Execution, letting the Activity resume from a checkpoint rather than restarting from scratch — this is the only mechanism for mid-Activity checkpointing in Temporal.

Heartbeat details cause Temporal to skip the failed Activity Task and mark the Activity Execution as completed using the last heartbeat payload as the return value, avoiding a full retry of the Activity function.

Heartbeat details are appended to the Workflow Event History as separate events, and on retry the new Worker replays those events to reconstruct Activity state, the same way Workflow replay works for deterministic code.

questions.explanation: By default, when an Activity fails or times out, the next attempt starts from the very beginning of the Activity function — Temporal does not automatically checkpoint intermediate progress. The Heartbeat mechanism is specifically designed to solve this: each heartbeat call sends a payload to the Temporal Service, which stores it server-side. When the next Activity Task Execution begins, the SDK can call the 'get heartbeat details' API to retrieve the last successfully recorded payload. This lets the Activity skip already-completed work (e.g., resume a file upload from byte offset N). Option A is wrong — heartbeat details are not in Worker memory; they are durably stored by the server. Option C is wrong — heartbeat details are not treated as a return value; the Activity must still complete normally. Option D is wrong — Activity execution is intentionally non-deterministic and is not replayed the way Workflow code is; only the final result (or failure) is recorded in Event History as ActivityTaskCompleted/Failed events.

questions.question: An engineer cancels a running Workflow Execution that has a long-running Activity in progress. The Activity has been designed to clean up cloud resources on cancellation. In Temporal Web UI the Activity remains in 'Running' for several minutes after the cancellation is requested. What is the most likely reason the Activity is not receiving the cancellation signal promptly?

choices.text: Temporal sends cancellations only at Activity boundaries; the current Activity must complete or time out before cancellation is delivered to the next scheduled Activity in the Workflow.
The Workflow's CancellationType is set to ABANDON by default, so the Temporal Service closes the Workflow immediately without propagating a cancellation request to in-flight Activities.

The Activity is not calling Heartbeat, so the Temporal Service has no delivery channel through which to push the cancellation; the Activity will only receive it once the Start-To-Close timeout fires and the server sends a TIMED_OUT cancellation failure.
Cancellation requests are batched by the Temporal Service and delivered every 60 seconds to reduce load on Activity Workers, causing the observed delay in receiving the signal.

questions.explanation: Temporal delivers cancellation to a running Activity through the heartbeat mechanism. When an Activity calls Heartbeat, the Temporal Service uses the response to that call to convey that cancellation has been requested, raising a CancellationError (or equivalent) in the SDK. An Activity that never heartbeats has no mechanism to receive cancellation proactively — the server cannot push an interrupt into an executing function. In this scenario, the Activity will continue running until either it finishes naturally, or the Start-To-Close timeout fires, at which point the server records a timeout and the cancellation failure message will be 'TIMED_OUT'. This is why the documentation states: 'Activities must heartbeat to receive cancellations from a Temporal Service.' Option A is incorrect — cancellation is not deferred to the next Activity. Option B confuses Activity cancellation propagation settings with the separate concept of Workflow CancellationType. Option D fabricates a batching behavior that does not exist in Temporal.

choices.text: Temporal detects the Worker crash immediately via the heartbeat timeout and retries the Activity. Because the payment API already succeeded, the Activity will be executed again and the customer will be charged twice unless the `ChargeCard` Activity uses the Workflow Run ID combined with the Activity ID as an idempotency key when calling the external payment API.

Temporal detects the Worker crash immediately via the heartbeat timeout, marks the Activity as failed, and does not retry because the `StartToClose` timeout was not exceeded yet. The Workflow must issue an explicit compensation Activity to check whether the payment succeeded on the external system.

Temporal does not detect the Worker crash directly; instead it waits until the `StartToClose` timeout expires (30 seconds), then retries the Activity Task. Because the Activity has already executed against the payment API, the retry will charge the card again unless `ChargeCard` is idempotent—typically by forwarding a stable idempotency key (e.g., derived from Workflow Run ID + Activity ID) to the payment provider, which deduplicates the charge.

Since the Activity was heartbeating every 5 seconds, Temporal detects the crash within one heartbeat interval and retries the Activity. Global Namespaces prevent duplicate execution by replicating the completed Event History to the standby region, so no idempotency key is required at the payment API layer for intra-region retries.

questions.explanation:
Temporal cannot detect a Worker crash directly—it has no keep-alive connection to Workers. The only mechanism for detecting a stuck or dead Activity Task is the `StartToClose` timeout (or, if heartbeating is configured, the `HeartbeatTimeout`). In this scenario the Activity is NOT heartbeating inside `ChargeCard` itself—the code shown performs a single synchronous call and returns. Heartbeating is only useful if you call `RecordHeartbeat` inside a long-running loop; a single-call Activity that crashes mid-flight before returning simply waits out the full `StartToClose` timeout before Temporal retries it.

Once that timeout fires, the Activity Task is rescheduled and the function executes again from scratch. Since the external payment API already processed the charge, this retry will result in a duplicate charge unless the implementation forwards a stable idempotency key. The documentation explicitly recommends using `WorkflowRunID + ActivityID` as the idempotency key because that combination is stable across retries of the same Activity Execution but unique across different Workflow Executions.

Option A is wrong because Temporal only detects the crash via a *heartbeat timeout* if `RecordHeartbeat` is actually being called; a one-shot Activity has no heartbeat to time out—only `StartToClose` applies.

Option B is wrong because `StartToClose` timeout is precisely the trigger for retry, not a reason to skip it; the timeout expiring is what causes the retry attempt.

Option D is wrong on two fronts: heartbeating every 5 seconds only matters if the Activity code calls `RecordHeartbeat`, which this code does not; and Global Namespace replication prevents cross-region duplication but does nothing to prevent a local retry from re-hitting the payment API.

The production insight: any Activity that touches external systems must treat idempotency as a first-class concern at the *external API boundary*, not just within Temporal itself. Temporal guarantees exactly-once *observation* (the result appears in Event History once), but may execute the Activity function multiple times during that process.
